goal: Deliver a working clone of the POSIX `tail` utility, written in Rust.

mustHave: `tail <file>` — print the last 10 lines of a file (default behavior)
`tail -f <file>` — follow a file, printing appended data as it grows
Reading from stdin when no file argument is given
Support for multiple file arguments with headers between them
niceToHave: `tail -q` — suppress headers when multiple files are given

requirements: Create a gitea repository named `tail-rs` to host the project.
Implement in Rust using idiomatic patterns (clap for arg parsing is acceptable).
Use gitea issues for planning. Each issue should represent a single deliverable increment.
Work incrementally: each pull request should be small, focused, and independently testable.
Spawn an adversarial review agent for each pull request. The reviewer should look for correctness bugs, edge cases, and deviations from POSIX behavior.
operatingPrinciples: You are the orchestration agent. You plan and coordinate but never write code directly — you spawn subagents to implement, test, and review.
Break the work into issues before any code is written. Sequence issues so each builds on the last.
When a subagent gets stuck or produces poor results, re-scope the task rather than retrying the same prompt.
acceptanceCriteria: All mustHave features pass manual smoke tests against equivalent GNU tail output.
The binary compiles with `cargo build --release` with no warnings.
At least basic unit tests exist for line-counting and byte-counting logic.
`tail -f` correctly streams appended content without busy-waiting (use inotify or polling with sleep).
delivery: Create a gitea release tagged with a semver version (e.g., v0.1.0).
Attach the release-mode binary (linux/amd64) to the release.
The release description should summarize implemented features and known limitations.
